content.summary: Academic debate centers on whether separation of powers produces efficiency or gridlock, and how partisan polarization affects inter-branch relations.
key_points: Divided government historically common; unified government no guarantee of productivity
Judicial deference doctrines (Chevron, Auer) shift power to executive agencies
Partisan polarization increases inter-branch conflict regardless of institutional design
Emergency powers create temporary power concentration, often become permanent
Comparative studies show US system more fragmented than parliamentary systems
Oversight effectiveness varies with party control and media attention
sources: Jacob Hacker & Paul Pierson, American Amnesia (2016)
Comparative political science literature on institutional design
content.philosophy_guard_note: Descriptive claim about presidential systems; no normative advocacy regarding what should be
